data.title: Short interest across S&P 500 real estate stocks increase in November
data.description:
As of November end, average short interest across S&P 500 real estate stocks increased to 2.80% of shares float from 2.65% at the end of the prior month.

Short interest in a stock represents the number of shares sold short but not yet repurchased by the short seller. The metric is often expressed as a percentage of shares outstanding.

Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLRE) added 2.06% in November, against the broader S&P 500 index's 0.04% loss.

Industry analysis

Office REITs were the most shorted subsector in November. Average short interest across the property sector stood at 6.41%.

Hotel and Resort REITs took the second spot, with bets against the property sector coming at 5.12%.

Industrial REITs remained the least shorted subsector. Notably, short sellers increased their bets against the property sector to 1.27% from 1.04%.

Iron Mountain (IRM) saw the largest jump in short bets. IRM saw the metric increase to 3.38% from 2.62%. Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE) followed, with the November-end short interest increasing to 3.66% from 3.01%.

Host Hotels & Resorts (HST) saw the steepest decline in short bets to 5.19% from 6.05%.

Most shorted stocks

  • BXP (BXP) - 6.43% vs. 6.82% last month
  • Host Hotels & Resorts (HST) - 5.19% vs. 6.05% last month
  • Realty Income (O) - 5.14% vs. 4.80% last month
  • CoStar Group (CSGP) - 4.19% vs. 4.00% last month
  • Kimco Realty (KIM) - 3.78% vs. 3.38% last month

Least shorted stocks

  • Prologis (PLD) - 1.27% vs. 1.04% last month
  • American Tower (AMT) - 1.28% vs. 1.06% last month
  • CBRE Group (CBRE) - 1.35% vs. 1.07% last month
  • Simon Property Group (SPG) - 1.40% vs. 1.47% last month
  • Equity Residential (EQR) - 1.64% vs. 1.51% last month

data.title: Apollo, KKR wooed by Jared Kushner to join Ukraine’s postwar reconstruction: NYP
data.description:
President Trump’s son-in-law, Jared Kushner, a key figure in peace negotiations between Russia and Ukraine, has approached Apollo Global Management (APO) and KKR (KKR) seeking their support in post-war reconstruction of the war-torn country, The New York Post reported.

Citing sources familiar with the matter, the Post said that Kushner held initial discussions with top asset managers in Manhattan and conducted meetings with Apollo (APO) and KKR (KKR) at the offices of the private equity giants.

While the discussions are at a preliminary stage and may not succeed, the move could raise concerns among Ukraine’s European allies that they will be sidelined when it comes to any lucrative contracts for reconstruction, a source said.  

Kushner’s meetings with Apollo (APO) and KKR (KKR) came on the day the 44-year-old had a call with Larry Fink, CEO of BlackRock (BLK), under whose leadership the company halted a multi-billion-dollar Ukraine Development Fund in July amid uncertainty over how Trump would end the war.

Steve Witkoff, Trump’s Special Envoy to Ukraine, U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky also joined the discussions.

Moves to establish an investment fund focused on sectors such as rare metals sought by the U.S. have emerged as a key point in plans to rebuild Ukraine.

data.title: State of Alcohol: Consumption rates are down; innovation and M&A may be on the rise
data.description:
A lower share of U.S. adults now drinks alcohol, with the drinking rate at a multi-decade low and younger age groups in particular shifting away from regular consumption. The structural change is tied to evolving health perceptions, generational attitudes, and the growing popularity of no- and low-alcohol alternatives that continue to reshape beverage portfolios in the beer and spirits industry. 

Gallup polling from earlier in the year indicated that only about 54% of U.S. adults now say they consume alcohol, the lowest reading in roughly 90 years of the survey and down from the 60% to 70% range that has prevailed for most of the past few decades. The decline is broad-based across gender, race, and age cohorts, although the most dramatic behavioral shift is among adults under 35, where self-reported drinking has fallen sharply from earlier in the 2010s. Industry analysis indicates that drinkers report consuming fewer drinks and on fewer drinking occasions than in prior years, reinforcing the sense that it is a volume-light, frequency-light environment in the U.S. rather than simply a mix shift.

The most recent aggregated card data from Bank of America for spending in U.S. alcohol stores showed a year-over-year decline of 8.6% in November, which analyst Andrea Pistacchi noted was a deterioration versus October (-2.7%), on a more difficult comparable base. Dissecting the data by age group, spending in alcohol stores by Gen-Zs declined -8.3% in November vs. -8.8% for Millennials, -7.9% for Gen X,  and -7.2% for Baby Boomers.

Rising health and wellness consciousness has led a majority of Americans to acknowledge that even moderate drinking is bad for their health, a dramatic change from a decade ago when far fewer associated "one or two drinks a day" with meaningful risk.  At the same time, economic pressure, "better-for-you" lifestyle branding, and the availability of credible non-alcoholic substitutes (from mocktails to adult spirits) make it easier for consumers to opt out. Social behaviors are also believed to be in play with the decreased alcohol consumption rate. An increase in average time spent online, a pivot toward earlier, activity-based socializing, and the normalization of Dry January–style challenges are all considered factors.

For investors, the downshift in alcohol incidence and intensity has implications across a wide set of companies, such as Anheuser-Busch InBev (BUD), Molson Coors (TAP), Boston Beer (TAP), Constellation Brands (STZ), Diageo (DEO), Brown-Forman (BF.A) (BF.B), Willamette Valley Vineyards (WVVI), Crimson Wine Group (CWGL), Heineken (HEINY), Pernod Ricard (PRDRDF) (PRNDY), Campari (DVDCF) (DVCMY), Remy Cointreau (REMYF) (REMYY), and Carlsberg (CABGY). While Europe still has the highest alcohol consumption rate across major regions, there are health concerns, tighter policies in some nations, and growing availability of alcohol-free beer and spirits changing behavior. Meanwhile, Asia has seen a general trend toward lower alcohol consumption, although India and some Southeast nations are still growing from a lower base.

An interesting development in the last few years is that large non-alcoholic beverage and consumer packaged players such as Coca-Cola (KO), PepsiCo (PEP), Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP), and Monster Beverage (MNST) are edging into the alcohol space. Coca-Cola (KO) and PepsiCo (PEP) have direct co-branded alcoholic RTDs via partnerships, Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP) is focused on non-alcoholic cocktails, and Monster's (MNST) alcohol moves are via acquired brands and lines rather than its core energy franchise. At the same time, large beer companies like Anheuser-Busch (BUD) are blurring the lines by getting into the energy drink space. Looking ahead to 2026, Wall Street analysts expect M&A to move back to the forefront, perhaps even a blockbuster deal.

data.title: Insider trades: Nvidia, Salesforce, AMD among notable names this week
data.description:
This week, insider trades were seen across tech heavyweights like Nvidia (NVDA), Salesforce (CRM) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). Below is a list of transactions that occurred between December 8 and December 12.

  • Several Nvidia (NVDA) insiders divested their stakes in the company this week. While most others were done under a trading plan, Director Mark Stevens sold a stake worth $63.6M. He let go of 350,000 shares of the company at an average price of $181.73 per share. Following the transaction, Stevens now holds roughly 34.66 million Nvidia shares.
  • Advanced Micro Devices’ (AMD) Executive Vice President and Chief Sales Officer Darren Grasby sold 10,000 shares at $218.77 each, for a total worth of $2.19M. After the sale, Grasby continues to own about 133,344 AMD shares.
  • Denis Oleary, director at CrowdStrike (CRWD), sold 7,750 shares of the company in the price range of $515.31 to $516.33, for a total value of roughly $4.0M. Oleary now holds 94,147 shares in the company.
  • Palo Alto Networks’ (PANW) Director James Goetz sold a combined 25,000 shares across several transactions within a price range of $194.7 and $196.42 per share, for total proceeds of about $4.88M. Following these sales, Goetz owns about 377,264 shares in the firm.
  • Home Depot (HD) saw an insider sell shares for the first time since September. Senior Executive Vice President Ann Marie Campbell cut down a stake worth 51,948 in the firm. Campbell sold 145 shares at $358.26 each, decreasing her holding to 12,320 shares.
  • On the buy side, Salesforce’s (CRM) Director Mason Morfit purchased 96,000 shares of the company at an average price of $260.58 per share, for a total value of about $25.0M. Following the transaction, Morfit holds around 2.99 million shares in the firm.
  • Lyft’s (LYFT) Chief Executive Officer David Risher bought 5,030 shares at $19.80 each, totaling roughly $99.6K. Risher now owns about 11.8 million Lyft shares.
  • Linde’s (LIN) Chief Executive Officer, Sanjiv Lamba, acquired 2,520 shares of the company at a price of $396.68 per share, for a total value of about $1.0M. Lamba’s holdings in the company have now increased to 90,794 shares.
  • Norfolk Southern’s (NSC) Director William Clyburn purchased 204 shares at $294.79 each, for a total value of roughly $60.1K. Clyburn now owns 405 Norfolk Southern shares.

data.title: J&J loses latest talc case; ordered to pay $40M to two women
data.description:
• A California jury on Friday found in favor of two women who sued Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), alleging that the healthcare giant's baby powder, which contained talc, caused their ovarian cancer.
  • J&J was ordered to pay $18M to one of the women, and $22M to the other.
  • The company told Reuters it plans to appeal. 
  • Both women said they used J&J's baby powder for about 40 years. Their lawyers argued the company knew about risks associated with the product for decades but kept quiet.

data.title: Intel said to be nearing $1.6B deal to buy AI chip startup SambaNova
data.description:
Intel (INTC) is in advanced discussion with privately owned SambaNova Systems Inc. to acquire the artificial intelligence chip startup for roughly $1.6B, including debt, Bloomberg News reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

The parties could reach a buyout agreement as early as January, the people said, asking for anonymity because the information is not yet public. 

Terms and timing of the deal could change, according to the people, who added that SambaNova, which has signed term sheets with other potential investors, could seek another strategic direction.

Intel (INTC) CEO Lip-Bu Tan serves as chairman of Palo Alto, California-based SambaNova, which designs custom AI chips in a market dominated by Nvidia (NVDA).

A potential acquisition is expected to give Intel (INTC) a much sought-after platform to help build out its AI portfolio at a discount, after SambaNova drew a $5B valuation in 2021 as part of a funding round led by SoftBank Group’s (SFTBY) (SFTBF) Vision Fund 2.

data.title: House Republicans introduce health care bill without enhanced subsidies
data.description:
A Republican health care bill introduced in the House Friday evening would not extend expiring enhanced ACA subsidies -- a measure being demanded by Democrats  -- but instead allow employers to work with others across industries to purchase group health plans together and mandate greater transparency from pharmacy benefit managers, the so-called "drug middlemen."

GOP leaders, in a concession to moderates in its party, will allow a floor vote to extend the enhanced subsidies, Politico reported.

The new legislation, dubbed The Lower Health Care Premium for All Americans Act and introduced by Rep. Mariannette Miller-Meeks (R-Iowa), would provide funding for cost sharing reduction staring in 2027, "lowering premiums and stabilizing the individual market while ensuring taxpayer dollars are used responsibly," a news release from Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) stated.

On the PBM front, the bill would require them to provide employers with data on prescription drug spending, rebates, spread pricing, and formulary choices.

Accessibility to Association Health Plans would also increase. Currently, these plans are only available to employers in the same trade, industry, or profession, with no options for the self-employed. The legislation would change this by allowing employers irrespective of industry or profession to join together, and also allow the self-employed to take part. 

In addition, it would formalize a concept known as Custom Health Option and Individual Care Expense (CHOICE) arrangement permiting employers to give money to employees to purchase their own coverage and pay those premiums pre-tax.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Enova, eToro, NP, SLM among week's biggest financials movers
data.description:
Financial stocks (XLF) largely drifted higher, while the broader S&P 500 slipped in the past week, which was marked by the Federal Reserve's third consecutive interest-rate cut.

Enova International (ENVA) rose the most of any financial stock (with market cap over $2B) in the week ended Dec. 12, 2025, jumping 20% after announcing a $369M deal to buy Grasshopper Bancorp;

Neptune Insurance Holdings (NP) gapped up 12%;

Slide Insurance Holdings (SLDE) climbed 11%;

American International Group (AIG) gained 10%; and

HCI Group (HCI) rounded out the five biggest winners -- mostly comprising insurance companies -- with a 10.2% rise.

On the losers, Sallie Mae (SLM) retreated 12% after the student debt lender introduced below-consensus earnings estimates during its 2025 investor day forum after Monday's close;

eToro Group (ETOR) slid 11.8%;

Cipher Mining (CIFR) shed 11.6%;

IREN (IREN) fell 10%; and

Robinhood Markets (HOOD), which during the week posted disappointing trading volumes for November, gapped down 9.4%.

data.title: SpaceX valued at $800B in share sale ahead of potential 2026 IPO
data.description:
Ahead of a possible IPO in 2026, billionaire Elon Musk’s rocket and satellite maker SpaceX (SPACE) has commenced a secondary share sale that would value the company at $800B, Reuters reported, citing a shareholder sent by CFO Bret Johnsen.

In a letter dated Dec. 12, Johnsen confirmed plans for the company’s IPO and announced an arrangement with new and existing investors to buy SpaceX (SPACE) shares from eligible shareholders at $421 apiece.

“We are preparing the company for a possible IPO in 2026,” he said, confirming CEO Musk’s prior remarks about the public listing.

“Whether it actually happens, when it happens, and at what valuation are still highly uncertain, but the thinking is that if we execute brilliantly and the markets cooperate, a public offering could raise a significant amount of capital,” Johnsen added.

The IPO, which could rank among the biggest public listings in the world, is expected to raise funds for the company’s plans to establish AI data centers in space, send uncrewed and crewed missions to Mars, and other ambitions, according to him.

data.title: Apollo reportedly bet against debt of software makers exposed to AI
data.description:
Apollo Global Management (APO) has taken bearish bets on several large loans held by software makers vulnerable to artificial intelligence and reduced its credit exposure to the sector, The Financial Times reported on Saturday.

Companies including Internet Brands, SonicWall, and Perforce, owned by large private equity firms KKR (KKR), Francisco Partners, and Clearlake, respectively, were subject to APO’s bearish bets, according to people briefed on the matter.

The bets made up less than 1% of the private capital giant’s overall $700B in credit assets, one person said, while another added that they lasted through much of 2025 but have since been closed.

It was not immediately clear the exact size and the returns of the short positions, and having sold off several times this year, the loans Apollo (APO) bet against are now trading above 80 cents on the dollar.

According to the FT, the company has previously determined AI as a potential opportunity for software companies, with many of its private credit funds holding about 20% of exposure to the sector at the start of this year.

Since then, Apolo (APO) has cut its investments in the sector by almost half, with its top leadership recommending against directional industry bets and deciding to reduce exposure.

“Technology change is going to cause massive dislocation in the credit market,” CEO Marc Rowan said at a recent conference. “I don’t know whether that’s going to be enterprise software, which could […] benefit or be destroyed by this. As a lender, I’m not sure I want to be there to find out.”
